entry.title: [Optimization] Approximation and Fitting
entry.content:
Norm Approximation

Problem Definition

We are considering

\[\min_{\mathbf{x}} \|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|\]

where $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ with $m\geq n$, and $|\cdot |$ is a norm on $\mathbb{R}^m $. This can be considered as a convex optimization problem, since norm is a convex function, and $\mathbf{Ax-b}$ is an affine transformation.

Interpretation

We can interpret this problem in many ways:

  1. geometric : $\mathbf{Ax}^\star$ is a point in $\mathcal{R}(\mathbf{A})$ closest to $\mathbf{b}$.

  2. estimation : For a linear measurement model

    \[\mathbf{y = Ax+v}\]

    where $\mathbf{y}$ is the observation, $\mathbf{x}$ is unknown, and $\mathbf{v}$ is the measurement error, given $\mathbf{y=b}$, $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is the best guess of $\mathbf{x}$.

  3. optimal design : $\mathbf{x}$ is the design variable, where $\mathbf{Ax}$ is the result. $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is the design choice that best approximates the desired result $\mathbf{b}$.

Example

  • least-squares approximation $| \cdot |_2$ :
\[\begin{aligned} \min_{\mathbf{x}} & \ \ \ \|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|_2^2 = \mathbf{(Ax-b)}^T\mathbf{(Ax-b)}\\ & \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ = \mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}-2\mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{b} \end{aligned}\]

the solution satisfies the normal equation

\[\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{Ax} = \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}\]

If $\text{rank } \mathbf{A}=n$ , $\mathbf{x}^\star = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}$

  • chebysev approximation $|\cdot |_\infty$ :
\[\begin{aligned} \min_{\mathbf{x}} & \ \ \ \bigg ( \|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|_\infty = \max_i\ \big | \mathbf{a}_i^T \mathbf{x}-\mathbf{b}_i \big | \bigg ) \end{aligned}\]

this can be converted to LP

\[\begin{aligned} \min & \ \ \ t \\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ \ |\mathbf{a}_i ^T \mathbf{x}- \mathbf{b}_i |\leq t, \ \ \ i = 1,\cdots, m\\ & \ \ \ \iff -t\mathbf{1} \leq \mathbf{Ax-b} \leq t\mathbf{1} \end{aligned}\]
  • sum of absolute residuals approximation $|\cdot |_1 $ :
\[\begin{aligned} \min_{\mathbf{x}} & \ \ \ \bigg ( \|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|_1 = \sum_i \ \big | \mathbf{a}_i^T \mathbf{x}-\mathbf{b}_i \big | \bigg ) \end{aligned}\]

this can be converted to LP

\[\begin{aligned} \min & \ \ \ \mathbf{1}^T \mathbf{y} \\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ \ |\mathbf{A} \mathbf{x}- \mathbf{b} |\leq \mathbf{y}\\ & \ \ \ \iff -\mathbf{y} \leq \mathbf{Ax-b} \leq \mathbf{y} \end{aligned}\]

Penalty Function Approximation

Problem Defintion

In $l_p$-norm approximation, for $1\leq p< \infty$, the objective is

\[\big( |\mathbf{r}_1|^p+|\mathbf{r}_2|^p+ \cdots + |\mathbf{r}_m|^p\big ) ^{1/p}\]

where

\[\mathbf{r}_i = \mathbf{a}_i ^T \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}_i\]

In that case, if we define $\phi: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$,

\[\phi(r) = |r|^p\]

we can convert above problem into

\[\begin{aligned} \min & \ \ \ \sum_{i=1}^m \phi (\mathbf{r}_i )\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ \ \mathbf{r= Ax-b} \end{aligned}\]

We call $\phi$ a convex penalty function.

Examples

  • Quadratic : \(\phi(u) = u^2\)

  • Deadzone-linear with width $a$ : \(\phi(u) = \max \{0,\vert u\vert-a\}\)

  • Log-barrier with limit $a$ : \(\phi(u)= \begin{cases} -a^2 \log(1- (u/a)^2 ) & \ \text{if } |u| <a\\ \infty & \ \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\)

Huber penalty function

\[\phi_{\text{hub}}(u) = \begin{cases} u^2 & \ \text{if } |u| \leq M\\ M(2|u|-M)& \ \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\]

This huber penalty function allows an approximation less sensitive to outliers. In case of a least-squares regression where we fit $(t,y)$ with a linear curve $y = \alpha + \beta t$, using a huber penalty function makes us to draw a line that’s way more robust to outliers.

\[\min \sum_i \phi_{\text{hub}}(y_i - \alpha- \beta t_i )\]

Least-Norm Problem

Problem Definition

Consider the problem

\[\begin{aligned} \min_{\mathbf{x}} & \ \ \|\mathbf{x}\|\\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ \mathbf{Ax=b} \end{aligned}\]

where $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with $m\leq n$ (rows of $\mathbf{A}$ are linearly independent) and $|\cdot |$ is a norm on $\mathbb{R}^m $. In case of $m=n$, $\mathbf{x}^\star = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$. Thus, we assume $m<n$.

Interpretation

  1. geometric : $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is a point in the affine set \(\{\mathbf{x} \vert \mathbf{Ax=b}\}\) with minimum distance to origin $\mathbf{0}$.
  2. estimation : $\mathbf{b=Ax}$ are perfect measurements of $\mathbf{x}$. Among those, $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is the smallest estimate, consistent with the measurements.
  3. optimal design : $\mathbf{x}$ are design variables, $\mathbf{b}$ are required results: $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is the most efficient design that satisfies the requirements.

Examples

  • In case of $|\cdot |_2$, this problem can be solved by optimality conditions

    \[\begin{aligned} \min _\mathbf{x} & \ \ \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 ^2 = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x}\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ \mathbf{Ax=b} \end{aligned}\]

    where the Lagrangian is defined by

    \[\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x, \nu}) = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x} + \nu^T (\mathbf{Ax-b})\]

    Then, if we assume $(\mathbf{x}^\star, \nu^\star)$ as a primal and dual optimal point, below conditions must hold.

    \[2\mathbf{x}^\star+ \mathbf{A}^T \nu ^\star = \mathbf{0}, \ \ \mathbf{Ax}^\star =b\]

    From these, we have

    \[2\mathbf{Ax}^\star + \mathbf{AA}^T \mathbf{\nu}^\star = \mathbf{0}\\ \iff 2\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{AA}^T \mathbf{\nu}^\star =0 \\ \iff \nu^\star = -2(\mathbf{AA}^T)^{-1}\mathbf{b}\]

    and

    \[\mathbf{x}^\star = \mathbf{A}^T (\mathbf{AA}^T)^{-1}\mathbf{b}\]

    Note that $\text{rank } \mathbf{A} = m <n$ , and thus the matrix $\mathbf{AA}^T$ is invertible.

  • In case of $|\cdot |_1 $, this can be solved by LP

    \[\begin{aligned} \min _\mathbf{x} & \ \ \mathbf{1}^T \mathbf{y}\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ \mathbf{Ax=b}\\ & \ \ -\mathbf{y}\leq \mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{y} \end{aligned}\]

Regularized Approximation

Problem Definiion

Regularization is a common scalariziation method used to solve the bi-criterion problem. One form of regularization is to minimize the weighted sum of the objectives:

\[\min \|\mathbf{Ax-b}\| + \gamma \|\mathbf{x}\|\]

where $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ and norms on $\mathbb{R}^m$ and $\mathbb{R}^n$ can be different.

Tikhonov regularization

The most common form of regularization is the one with Euclidean norms, resulting in a conevx quadratic optimization problem.

\[\min \|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|_2 ^2 + \delta \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 ^2 = \mathbf{x}^T (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}+ \delta \mathbf{I})\mathbf{x}- 2\mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{Ax}+ \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{b}\]

This has an analytical solution of

\[\mathbf{x}^\star = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}+ \delta \mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}\]

Optimal Input Design

Consider a linear dynamical system with input $u(t)$ and output $y(t)$.

  1. tracking error with desired output $y_{des}$:

    \[J_{track} := \sum_{t=0}^N (y(t) - y_{des}(t))^2\]

  2. input variation :

    \[J_{der} := \sum_{t=0}^{N-1} (u(t+1)- u(t))^2\]

  3. input magnitude :

    \[J_{mag} := \sum_{t=0}^N u(t)^2\]

Regularized least-squares formulation:

\[\min_u J_{track} + \delta J_{der} + \eta J_{mag}\]

Signal Reconstruction

Problem Definition

Consider the multi-objective problem

\[\min_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}} \ (\|\hat{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{x}_{cor} \|_2 , \phi(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) )\]

where $\mathbf{x}$ is unknown signal, and $\mathbf{x}_{cor} = \mathbf{x}+\mathbf{v}$ is observed corrupted version of $\mathbf{x}$, with additive noise $\mathbf{v}$. $\phi$ is regularization function or smoothing objective.

Example

  • Quadratic smoothing :

    \[\phi_{quad}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} (\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{i+1} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i )^2\]

  • Total variation smoothing :

    \[\phi_{tv}(\hat {\mathbf{x}}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1}|\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{i+1} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i |\]

Total variation vs Quadratic Smoothing

Total variation smoothing preserves sharp transitions in signal!

Robust Approximation

Problem Definition

We want to minimize \(\|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|\) with uncertain \(\mathbf{A}\). We can take two approaches:

  1. Stochastic : assume $\mathbf{A}$ is random, and minimize \(\mathbb{E}\|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|\).
  2. Worst-case : choose set of $\mathcal{A}$ of possible values of $\mathbf{A}$, and minimize the worst-case scenario, \(\sup_{\mathbf{A}\in \mathcal{A}} \|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|\).

Example

Let $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{A}_0 + u \mathbf{A}_1 $. Stochastic case minimizes

\[\mathbb{E} \|\mathbf{A}(u)\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}\|_2 ^2 , \ \ u \in \text{uni}[-1,1]\]

Worst-case minimizes

\[\sup_{u\in [-1,1]} \|\mathbf{A}(u) \mathbf{x}- \mathbf{b}\|_2^2\]

The residual in three cases \(r(u) = \|\mathbf{A}(u)\mathbf{x}- \mathbf{b}\|_2\)

Stochastic Robust Least Squares

Let $\mathbf{A = \bar{A}+U}$, where $\mathbf{U}$ is random with $\mathbb{E}\mathbf{U}=\mathbf{0}$, and $\mathbb{E}\mathbf{U}^T \mathbf{U = P}$. Consider the problem

\[\min \ \mathbb{E}\|\mathbf{(\bar{A}+U)x-b}\|_2 ^2\]

This can be converted to the problem of

\[\mathbb{E} \|\mathbf{(\bar{A} + U)x - b}\|_2 ^2 =\|\mathbf{\bar{A}x-b}\|_2 ^2 + \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Px}\]

Hence, this problem is equivalent to LS problem

\[\mathbb{E} \|\mathbf{(\bar{A} + U)x - b}\|_2 ^2 =\|\mathbf{\bar{A}x-b}\|_2 ^2 +\|\mathbf{P}^{1/2}\mathbf{x}\|_2^2\]

for \(\mathbf{P} = \delta \mathbf{I}\), we obtain a Tikhonov regularized problem.

References

  • S. Boyd, L.Vandenberghe, Convex Opimization, Cambridge University Press, 2004. http://stanford.edu/~boyd/cvxbook/
  • Optimization Theory and Applications, SNU EE 2021, Insoon Yang

u^\star = -2(\mathbf{AA}^T)^{-1}\mathbf{b}\] and \[\mathbf{x}^\star = \mathbf{A}^T (\mathbf{AA}^T)^{-1}\mathbf{b}\] Note that $	ext{rank } \mathbf{A} = m <n$ , and thus the matrix $\mathbf{AA}^T$ is invertible. Tikhonov regularization The most common form of regularization is the one with Euclidean norms, resulting in a conevx quadratic optimization problem. \[\min \|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|_2 ^2 + \delta \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 ^2 = \mathbf{x}^T (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}+ \delta \mathbf{I})\mathbf{x}- 2\mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{Ax}+ \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{b}\] This has an analytical solution of \[\mathbf{x}^\star = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}+ \delta \mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}\] Optimal Input Design Consider a linear dynamical system with input $u(t)$ and output $y(t)$. tracking error with desired output $y_{des}$: \[J_{track} := \sum_{t=0}^N (y(t) - y_{des}(t))^2\] input variation : \[J_{der} := \sum_{t=0}^{N-1} (u(t+1)- u(t))^2\] input magnitude : \[J_{mag} := \sum_{t=0}^N u(t)^2\] Regularized least-squares formulation: \[\min_u J_{track} + \delta J_{der} + \eta J_{mag}\] Signal Reconstruction Problem Definition Consider the multi-objective problem \[\min_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}} \ (\|\hat{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{x}_{cor} \|_2 , \phi(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) )\] where $\mathbf{x}$ is unknown signal, and $\mathbf{x}_{cor} = \mathbf{x}+\mathbf{v}$ is observed corrupted version of $\mathbf{x}$, with additive noise $\mathbf{v}$. $\phi$ is regularization function or smoothing objective. Example Quadratic smoothing : \[\phi_{quad}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} (\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{i+1} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i )^2\] Total variation smoothing : \[\phi_{tv}(\hat {\mathbf{x}}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1}|\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{i+1} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i |\] Total variation vs Quadratic Smoothing Total variation smoothing preserves sharp transitions in signal! Robust Approximation Problem Definition We want to minimize \(\|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|\) with uncertain \(\mathbf{A}\). We can take two approaches: Stochastic : assume $\mathbf{A}$ is random, and minimize \(\mathbb{E}\|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|\). Worst-case : choose set of $\mathcal{A}$ of possible values of $\mathbf{A}$, and minimize the worst-case scenario, \(\sup_{\mathbf{A}\in \mathcal{A}} \|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|\). Example Let $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{A}_0 + u \mathbf{A}_1 $. Stochastic case minimizes \[\mathbb{E} \|\mathbf{A}(u)\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}\|_2 ^2 , \ \ u \in 	ext{uni}[-1,1]\] Worst-case minimizes \[\sup_{u\in [-1,1]} \|\mathbf{A}(u) \mathbf{x}- \mathbf{b}\|_2^2\] The residual in three cases \(r(u) = \|\mathbf{A}(u)\mathbf{x}- \mathbf{b}\|_2\) Stochastic Robust Least Squares Let $\mathbf{A =  ar{A}+U}$, where $\mathbf{U}$ is random with $\mathbb{E}\mathbf{U}=\mathbf{0}$, and $\mathbb{E}\mathbf{U}^T \mathbf{U = P}$. Consider the problem \[\min \ \mathbb{E}\|\mathbf{( ar{A}+U)x-b}\|_2 ^2\] This can be converted to the problem of \[\mathbb{E} \|\mathbf{( ar{A} + U)x - b}\|_2 ^2 =\|\mathbf{ ar{A}x-b}\|_2 ^2 + \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Px}\] Hence, this problem is equivalent to LS problem \[\mathbb{E} \|\mathbf{( ar{A} + U)x - b}\|_2 ^2 =\|\mathbf{ ar{A}x-b}\|_2 ^2 +\|\mathbf{P}^{1/2}\mathbf{x}\|_2^2\] for \(\mathbf{P} = \delta \mathbf{I}\), we obtain a Tikhonov regularized problem. entry.title: [RL Theory] Temporal Difference Learning
entry.content:
Review : Fixed Point Problems

Bellman Equations

Recall, that both policy evaluation and optimality equation is a fixed point problem.

  • Policy Evaluation

    \[v = \mathcal{T}^\pi v\]

    or equivalently

\[v(s) = r(s, \pi(s)) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \vert s, \pi(s) ) v(s')\]
  • Optimality Equation
\[v = \mathcal{T}v\]

or equivalently

\[v(s) = \max_{a\in A} \bigg [ r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \vert s,a) v(s') \bigg ]\]

Thus, we need to solve a fixed-point problem in a model-free manner. Today, we’ll try to evaluate a policy in a model-free manner.

Naive : Monte Carlo Methods

Monte Carlo Approximation

  1. Choose sufficiently large $T$ so that $\gamma^T$ is negligible.

  2. Then, generate total $M$ state trajectories, each corresponding to $T+1$ number of states. For instance, $m$th trajectory will be

    \[\{s_t^{(m)} \}_{t=0}^T = \{s_0^{(m)}, s_1^{(m)}, \cdots, s_T^{(m)}\}\]

  3. Then, the total (discounted) cost incurred along this $m$th trajectory starting from $x_0$ is given by

    \[C^\pi (x_0, m) := \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \gamma^t r\big (s_t ^{(m)}, \pi(s_t ^{(m)})\big )\]

  4. Finally, we approximate the value function for policy $\pi$.

    \[v^\pi(x_0 ) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^M c^\pi (x_0, m)\]

Iterative Algorithm

  1. Fix a control policy $\pi$ (the policy we want to evaluate) and an initial state $s$.

  2. Initialize the value function $v(s) \leftarrow 0$

  3. Update the value as

    \(v(s) \leftarrow v(s) + \alpha_m (c_s (x,m) - v(s) ) , \ \ \ m=1, \cdots, M\) where the step size is given by

    \[\alpha_m = \frac{1}{M}\]

This is a model-free algorithm that can estimate the value function without model information, but it’s not an online algorithm. (We need at least one trajectory with $T+1$ states to perform one step of iteration.)

Temporal Difference (TD) Learning

Motivation

우리가 원하는 것은 episode 별로, 다시 말해 여러 개의 state transition을 모아서 $v^\pi$를 update하는 것이 아니라, state transition 을 한번 얻을 때마다 $v^\pi$를 update 하는 것. What we want is to update the value function $v^\pi$ after a single state transition, not an entire episode! To do so, we first define transition data under $\pi$.

\[\{ (s_t, a_t, r_t, s_{t+1})\}\]

Review : Stochastic Approximation Algorithm

Previous article dealt with Stochastic Approximation algorithm, trying to solve the following equation

\[Hx = x\]

by an iterative procedure

\[x_{k+1}(i) = (1-\alpha_k (i) )x_k (i) + \alpha_k (i) [(Hx_k) (i) + w_k (i) ].\]

We know that this converges if the following conditions hold.

  1. $\sum_{k=0}^\infty \alpha_k (i) = +\infty$
  2. $\sum_{k=0}^\infty \alpha_k ^2 (i) <+\infty$
  3. $\mathbb{E}[ w_k (i) \ \vert \ \mathcal{F}_k ] =0$
  4. $\mathbb{E}[ w_k^2 (i) \ \vert \mathcal{F}_k ] \leq C_0 + C_1 |x_k |^2$

TD(0): Stochastic Approximation to Policy Evaluation

Recall, our policy evaluation equation is

\[\begin{aligned} v^\pi (s_t) & = r(s_t,\pi(s_t)) + \gamma \sum_{s_{t+1}\in S} p(s_{t+1} \vert s_t,\pi(s_t)) v^\pi (s_{t+1})\\ & = r(s_t,\pi(s_t)) + \gamma \ \mathbb{E}\Big [ v^\pi (s_{t+1}) \Big ]\\ & = \mathbb{E} \Big [ r(s_t, \pi(s_t) ) + \gamma v^\pi (s_{t+1}) \Big ] \end{aligned}\]

and we are observing the transition (under policy $\pi$)

\[s_t, \ \ \ a_t ( = \pi(s_t) ), \ \ \ s_{t+1}, \ \ \ r_t ( = r(s_t, \pi(s_t) )\]

Recall that the simpliest Robbins-Monro stochastic approximation algorithm updates the value function using a single estimate

\[v^\pi (s_t) \leftarrow (1-\alpha_t ) v^\pi (s_t ) + \alpha_t \Big [ r_t + \gamma v^\pi (s_{t+1}) \Big ]\]

which can be rewritten as

\[v^\pi (s_t ) \leftarrow v^\pi (s_t) + \alpha_t \bigg [ r_t + \gamma v^\pi (s_{t+1}) - v^\pi (s_t) \bigg ]\]

We call the last term temporal difference:

\[\text{temporal difference} = d_t = r_t + \gamma v^\pi (s_{t+1}) - v^\pi (s_t)\]

As you can see, this TD(0) algorithm updates the value function $v$ at state $s_t$ only at the time when our trajectory visits the state $s_t$. Since we are not using an approximating function of value function $v^\pi$, we need a lookup table of $v$.

At this point, one might wonder: Why TD(0)? In fact, this is the lightest version of temporal difference learning, depending only on a single state transition ${s_t, a_t, s_{t+1}, r_t }$.

Multi-Step Generalization

First of all, let’s consider multi-step Bellman equation:

\[v^\pi (s_t ) = \mathbb{E} ^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=0}^l \gamma ^k r(s_{t+k}, \pi(s_{t+k}) ) + \gamma ^{l+1}v^\pi (s_{t+l+1})\ \Big \vert \ \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ]\]

You can see that we are considering $(l+1)$ steps ahead, and leaving the steps after $t+l+1$ for the dynamic programming. Now, let’s consider a weighted average of the multistep Bellman equations:

\[(1-\lambda) \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l v(s_t) = v(s_t)\]

We can then play with some algebra:

\[\begin{aligned} v(s_t) & = (1-\lambda) \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l v(s_t) \\ & = (1-\lambda) \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=0}^l \gamma ^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k} ) + \gamma ^{l+1}v (s_{t+l+1}) \Big \vert \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] \\ & = (1-\lambda) \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l \sum_{k=0}^l \bigg ( \gamma ^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k} ) \bigg )+ \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l \gamma ^{l+1}v (s_{t+l+1}) \Big \vert \mathcal{F}_t \bigg] \\ & = (1-\lambda) \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [\sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l \sum_{k=0}^l \gamma ^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k} ) + \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l \gamma ^{l+1}v (s_{t+l+1}) \Big \vert \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] \\ \end{aligned}\]

At this point, let’s brake this up a bit.

\[\begin{aligned} \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda ^l \sum_{k=0}^l \gamma^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k}) & = r(s_t,a_t) + \lambda \big ( r(s_t, a_t) + \gamma r(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1}) \big ) + \\ & \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ + \lambda^2 \big ( r(s_t, a_t) + \gamma r(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1})+ \gamma^2 (s_{t+2}, a_{t+2}) \big ) + \cdots \\ & = r(s_t, a_t) \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l + \gamma r(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1}) \sum_{l=1}^\infty \lambda^l + \gamma ^2 r(s_{t+2}, a_{t+2}) \sum_{l=2}^\infty \lambda^l + \cdots \\ & = \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k})\sum_{l=k}^\infty \lambda^l \end{aligned}\]

Let’s continue playing with some algebra:

\[\begin{aligned} v(s_t) & = (1-\lambda) \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [\sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l \sum_{k=0}^l \gamma ^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k} ) + \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l \gamma ^{l+1}v (s_{t+l+1}) \Big \vert \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] \\ & = (1-\lambda) \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k r(s_{t+k} , a_{t+k}) \sum_{l=k}^\infty \lambda^l + \sum_{l=0} ^\infty \lambda ^l \gamma ^{l+1} v(s_{t+l+1}) \ \vert \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] \\ & = \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k}) \sum_{l=k}^\infty (\lambda^l - \lambda^{l+1}) + \sum_{l=0}^\infty (\lambda^l - \lambda^{l+1}) \gamma ^{l+1} v(s_{t+l+1}) \vert \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] \\ & = \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma ^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k}) \lambda^k + \sum_{k=0}^\infty \lambda^k \gamma ^{k+1}v(s_{t+k+1}) - \sum_{k=0}^\infty \lambda^{k+1}\gamma^{k+1} v(s_{t+k+1}) \vert \mathcal{F}_k \bigg ] \\ & = \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma ^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k}) \lambda^k + \sum_{k=0}^\infty \lambda^k \gamma ^{k+1}v(s_{t+k+1}) - \sum_{k=0}^\infty \lambda^{k}\gamma^{k} v(s_{t+k}) \vert \mathcal{F}_k \bigg ] + v(s_k) \\ & = \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=0}^\infty(\lambda \gamma)^k \Big [ r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k}) + \gamma v(s_{t+k+1}) -v(s_{t+k}) \Big ] | \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] + v(s_k) \\ & = \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=t}^\infty (\lambda \gamma)^{k-t} \Big [ r(s_k, a_k) + \gamma v(s_{k+1}) - v(s_k) \Big ] | \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] + v(s_k)\\ & = \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=t}^\infty (\lambda \gamma)^{k-t} d_k | \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] + v(s_k)\\ \end{aligned}\]

Finally, our long-journey of algebra leads us to conclude that

\[\mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=t}^\infty (\lambda \gamma)^{k-t} d_k | \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] =0\]

where

\[d_k : = r(s_k, \pi(s_k) ) + \gamma v(s_{k+1}) - v(s_k)\]

This is important, since we require the noise $w_k$ to satisfy the zero-mean (bias) condition.

Thus, we can apply the Robbins-Monro stochastic approximation algorithm with step size $\alpha$.

\[v(s_t) \leftarrow (1-\alpha)v(s_t) + \alpha \bigg ( \sum_{k=t}^\infty (\lambda \gamma) ^{k-t} d_k + v(s_t) \bigg )\]

TD($\lambda$) Algorithm

  1. For a control policy $\pi$,

  2. Initialize $v=0$

  3. Using the trainsition data ${s_t, a_t, s_{t+1}, r_t ) }$, update $v(s_t)$.

    \[v(s_t) \leftarrow v(s_t) + \alpha_t \sum_{k=t}^\infty (\lambda \gamma) ^{k-t}d_t\]

Remarks on TD($\lambda$)

Extreme Cases

  • When $\lambda=0$, this corresponds to TD(0), only considering single sample for every iteration.

    \[v(s_t) \leftarrow v(s_t) + \alpha [ r_t + \gamma v(s_{t+1}) - v(s_t) ]\]

  • When $\lambda=1$, this corresponds to Monte Carlo Method, (for instance, by setting $t=0$)

    \[v(s_0) \leftarrow v(s_0) + \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k d_k\]

Bias-Variance Tradeoff

Suppose that we have a Markov chain, where two states 1 and 2 are adjacent such that $p_{12} >0$ and $p_{21} =0$. 2번 state로 한번 들어가면 다시는 1번 state로 돌아오지 못한다.

TD(1), the Monte-Carlo method, will try to use $c(1,m)$, the cumulative reward in the $m$-th trajectory starting from state 1 to estimate $v^\pi(1)$. Meanwhile, this $c(1,m)$ is an unbiased estimate of $v^\pi(1)$.

On the other hand, TD(0) uses the information $v^\pi(2)$ very efficiently while reducing the noise from the samples of $r_t$.

We can see that

  • Big $\lambda$ : small bias, large variance
  • Small $\lambda$ : large bias, small variance

Convergence of TD($\lambda$)

Theorem

Theorem Suppose that each state is visited infinitely often and that

\[\sum_{t=0}^\infty \alpha_t = +\infty, \ \ \ \sum_{t=0}^\infty \alpha_t^2 <+\infty\]

Then, for each $ s\in S$, the value $v(s)$ generated by TD($\lambda$) given a policy $\pi$ converges to $v^\pi(s)$, with probability 1.

Proof
entry.summary: Review : Fixed Point Problems Bellman Equations Recall, that both policy evaluation and optimality equation is a fixed point problem. Policy Evaluation \[v = \mathcal{T}^\pi v\] or equivalently \[v(s) = r(s, \pi(s)) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \vert s, \pi(s) ) v(s')\] Optimality Equation \[v = \mathcal{T}v\] or equivalently \[v(s) = \max_{a\in A} \bigg [ r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \vert s,a) v(s') \bigg ]\] Thus, we need to solve a fixed-point problem in a model-free manner. Today, we’ll try to evaluate a policy in a model-free manner. Naive : Monte Carlo Methods Monte Carlo Approximation Choose sufficiently large $T$ so that $\gamma^T$ is negligible. Then, generate total $M$ state trajectories, each corresponding to $T+1$ number of states. For instance, $m$th trajectory will be \[\{s_t^{(m)} \}_{t=0}^T = \{s_0^{(m)}, s_1^{(m)}, \cdots, s_T^{(m)}\}\] Then, the total (discounted) cost incurred along this $m$th trajectory starting from $x_0$ is given by \[C^\pi (x_0, m) := \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \gamma^t r\big (s_t ^{(m)}, \pi(s_t ^{(m)})\big )\] Finally, we approximate the value function for policy $\pi$. \[v^\pi(x_0 ) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^M c^\pi (x_0, m)\] Iterative Algorithm Fix a control policy $\pi$ (the policy we want to evaluate) and an initial state $s$. Initialize the value function $v(s) \leftarrow 0$ Update the value as \(v(s) \leftarrow v(s) + \alpha_m (c_s (x,m) - v(s) ) , \ \ \ m=1, \cdots, M\) where the step size is given by \[\alpha_m = \frac{1}{M}\] This is a model-free algorithm that can estimate the value function without model information, but it’s not an online algorithm. (We need at least one trajectory with $T+1$ states to perform one step of iteration.) Temporal Difference (TD) Learning Motivation 우리가 원하는 것은 episode 별로, 다시 말해 여러 개의 state transition을 모아서 $v^\pi$를 update하는 것이 아니라, state transition 을 한번 얻을 때마다 $v^\pi$를 update 하는 것. What we want is to update the value function $v^\pi$ after a single state transition, not an entire episode! To do so, we first define transition data under $\pi$. \[\{ (s_t, a_t, r_t, s_{t+1})\}\] Review : Stochastic Approximation Algorithm Previous article dealt with Stochastic Approximation algorithm, trying to solve the following equation \[Hx = x\] by an iterative procedure \[x_{k+1}(i) = (1-\alpha_k (i) )x_k (i) + \alpha_k (i) [(Hx_k) (i) + w_k (i) ].\] We know that this converges if the following conditions hold. $\sum_{k=0}^\infty \alpha_k (i) = +\infty$ $\sum_{k=0}^\infty \alpha_k ^2 (i) <+\infty$ $\mathbb{E}[ w_k (i) \ \vert \ \mathcal{F}_k ] =0$ $\mathbb{E}[ w_k^2 (i) \ \vert \mathcal{F}_k ] \leq C_0 + C_1 |x_k |^2$ TD(0): Stochastic Approximation to Policy Evaluation Recall, our policy evaluation equation is \[\begin{aligned} v^\pi (s_t) & = r(s_t,\pi(s_t)) + \gamma \sum_{s_{t+1}\in S} p(s_{t+1} \vert s_t,\pi(s_t)) v^\pi (s_{t+1})\\ & = r(s_t,\pi(s_t)) + \gamma \ \mathbb{E}\Big [ v^\pi (s_{t+1}) \Big ]\\ & = \mathbb{E} \Big [ r(s_t, \pi(s_t) ) + \gamma v^\pi (s_{t+1}) \Big ] \end{aligned}\] and we are observing the transition (under policy $\pi$) \[s_t, \ \ \ a_t ( = \pi(s_t) ), \ \ \ s_{t+1}, \ \ \ r_t ( = r(s_t, \pi(s_t) )\] Recall that the simpliest Robbins-Monro stochastic approximation algorithm updates the value function using a single estimate \[v^\pi (s_t) \leftarrow (1-\alpha_t ) v^\pi (s_t ) + \alpha_t \Big [ r_t + \gamma v^\pi (s_{t+1}) \Big ]\] which can be rewritten as \[v^\pi (s_t ) \leftarrow v^\pi (s_t) + \alpha_t \bigg [ r_t + \gamma v^\pi (s_{t+1}) - v^\pi (s_t) \bigg ]\] We call the last term temporal difference: \[\text{temporal difference} = d_t = r_t + \gamma v^\pi (s_{t+1}) - v^\pi (s_t)\] As you can see, this TD(0) algorithm updates the value function $v$ at state $s_t$ only at the time when our trajectory visits the state $s_t$. Since we are not using an approximating function of value function $v^\pi$, we need a lookup table of $v$. At this point, one might wonder: Why TD(0)? In fact, this is the lightest version of temporal difference learning, depending only on a single state transition ${s_t, a_t, s_{t+1}, r_t }$. Multi-Step Generalization First of all, let’s consider multi-step Bellman equation: \[v^\pi (s_t ) = \mathbb{E} ^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=0}^l \gamma ^k r(s_{t+k}, \pi(s_{t+k}) ) + \gamma ^{l+1}v^\pi (s_{t+l+1})\ \Big \vert \ \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ]\] You can see that we are considering $(l+1)$ steps ahead, and leaving the steps after $t+l+1$ for the dynamic programming. Now, let’s consider a weighted average of the multistep Bellman equations: \[(1-\lambda) \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l v(s_t) = v(s_t)\] We can then play with some algebra: \[\begin{aligned} v(s_t) & = (1-\lambda) \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l v(s_t) \\ & = (1-\lambda) \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=0}^l \gamma ^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k} ) + \gamma ^{l+1}v (s_{t+l+1}) \Big \vert \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] \\ & = (1-\lambda) \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l \sum_{k=0}^l \bigg ( \gamma ^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k} ) \bigg )+ \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l \gamma ^{l+1}v (s_{t+l+1}) \Big \vert \mathcal{F}_t \bigg] \\ & = (1-\lambda) \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [\sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l \sum_{k=0}^l \gamma ^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k} ) + \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l \gamma ^{l+1}v (s_{t+l+1}) \Big \vert \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] \\ \end{aligned}\] At this point, let’s brake this up a bit. \[\begin{aligned} \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda ^l \sum_{k=0}^l \gamma^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k}) & = r(s_t,a_t) + \lambda \big ( r(s_t, a_t) + \gamma r(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1}) \big ) + \\ & \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ + \lambda^2 \big ( r(s_t, a_t) + \gamma r(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1})+ \gamma^2 (s_{t+2}, a_{t+2}) \big ) + \cdots \\ & = r(s_t, a_t) \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l + \gamma r(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1}) \sum_{l=1}^\infty \lambda^l + \gamma ^2 r(s_{t+2}, a_{t+2}) \sum_{l=2}^\infty \lambda^l + \cdots \\ & = \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k})\sum_{l=k}^\infty \lambda^l \end{aligned}\] Let’s continue playing with some algebra: \[\begin{aligned} v(s_t) & = (1-\lambda) \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [\sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l \sum_{k=0}^l \gamma ^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k} ) + \sum_{l=0}^\infty \lambda^l \gamma ^{l+1}v (s_{t+l+1}) \Big \vert \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] \\ & = (1-\lambda) \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k r(s_{t+k} , a_{t+k}) \sum_{l=k}^\infty \lambda^l + \sum_{l=0} ^\infty \lambda ^l \gamma ^{l+1} v(s_{t+l+1}) \ \vert \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] \\ & = \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k}) \sum_{l=k}^\infty (\lambda^l - \lambda^{l+1}) + \sum_{l=0}^\infty (\lambda^l - \lambda^{l+1}) \gamma ^{l+1} v(s_{t+l+1}) \vert \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] \\ & = \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma ^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k}) \lambda^k + \sum_{k=0}^\infty \lambda^k \gamma ^{k+1}v(s_{t+k+1}) - \sum_{k=0}^\infty \lambda^{k+1}\gamma^{k+1} v(s_{t+k+1}) \vert \mathcal{F}_k \bigg ] \\ & = \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma ^k r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k}) \lambda^k + \sum_{k=0}^\infty \lambda^k \gamma ^{k+1}v(s_{t+k+1}) - \sum_{k=0}^\infty \lambda^{k}\gamma^{k} v(s_{t+k}) \vert \mathcal{F}_k \bigg ] + v(s_k) \\ & = \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=0}^\infty(\lambda \gamma)^k \Big [ r(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k}) + \gamma v(s_{t+k+1}) -v(s_{t+k}) \Big ] | \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] + v(s_k) \\ & = \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=t}^\infty (\lambda \gamma)^{k-t} \Big [ r(s_k, a_k) + \gamma v(s_{k+1}) - v(s_k) \Big ] | \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] + v(s_k)\\ & = \mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=t}^\infty (\lambda \gamma)^{k-t} d_k | \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] + v(s_k)\\ \end{aligned}\] Finally, our long-journey of algebra leads us to conclude that \[\mathbb{E}^\pi \bigg [ \sum_{k=t}^\infty (\lambda \gamma)^{k-t} d_k | \mathcal{F}_t \bigg ] =0\] where \[d_k : = r(s_k, \pi(s_k) ) + \gamma v(s_{k+1}) - v(s_k)\] This is important, since we require the noise $w_k$ to satisfy the zero-mean (bias) condition. Thus, we can apply the Robbins-Monro stochastic approximation algorithm with step size $\alpha$. \[v(s_t) \leftarrow (1-\alpha)v(s_t) + \alpha \bigg ( \sum_{k=t}^\infty (\lambda \gamma) ^{k-t} d_k + v(s_t) \bigg )\] TD($\lambda$) Algorithm For a control policy $\pi$, Initialize $v=0$ Using the trainsition data ${s_t, a_t, s_{t+1}, r_t ) }$, update $v(s_t)$. \[v(s_t) \leftarrow v(s_t) + \alpha_t \sum_{k=t}^\infty (\lambda \gamma) ^{k-t}d_t\] Remarks on TD($\lambda$) Extreme Cases When $\lambda=0$, this corresponds to TD(0), only considering single sample for every iteration. \[v(s_t) \leftarrow v(s_t) + \alpha [ r_t + \gamma v(s_{t+1}) - v(s_t) ]\] When $\lambda=1$, this corresponds to Monte Carlo Method, (for instance, by setting $t=0$) \[v(s_0) \leftarrow v(s_0) + \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k d_k\] Bias-Variance Tradeoff Suppose that we have a Markov chain, where two states 1 and 2 are adjacent such that $p_{12} >0$ and $p_{21} =0$. 2번 state로 한번 들어가면 다시는 1번 state로 돌아오지 못한다. TD(1), the Monte-Carlo method, will try to use $c(1,m)$, the cumulative reward in the $m$-th trajectory starting from state 1 to estimate $v^\pi(1)$. Meanwhile, this $c(1,m)$ is an unbiased estimate of $v^\pi(1)$. On the other hand, TD(0) uses the information $v^\pi(2)$ very efficiently while reducing the noise from the samples of $r_t$. We can see that Big $\lambda$ : small bias, large variance Small $\lambda$ : large bias, small variance Convergence of TD($\lambda$) Theorem Theorem Suppose that each state is visited infinitely often and that \[\sum_{t=0}^\infty \alpha_t = +\infty, \ \ \ \sum_{t=0}^\infty \alpha_t^2 <+\infty\] Then, for each $ s\in S$, the value $v(s)$ generated by TD($\lambda$) given a policy $\pi$ converges to $v^\pi(s)$, with probability 1. Proof

entry.title: [RL Theory] Stochastic Approximation
entry.content:
Review: MDP Problem

Bellman Equations

Recall, that both policy evaluation and optimality equation is a fixed point problem.

  • Policy Evaluation

    \[v = \mathcal{T}^\pi v\]

    or equivalently

\[v(s) = r(s, \pi(s)) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \vert s, \pi(s) ) v(s')\]

  • Optimality Equation

    \[v = \mathcal{T}v\]

    or equivalently

    \[v(s) = \max_{a\in A} \bigg [ r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \vert s,a) v(s') \bigg ]\]

Thus, we need to solve a fixed-point problem in a model-free manner.

Fixed Point Problem

Consider the following system of equations:

\[Hx = x\]

where $ H: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^n $.

We may want to use a deterministic iterative algorithm,

\[x_{k+1} := Hx_k\]

or its variant with a small stepsize $\alpha_k \in (0,1)$.

\[x_{k+1} := (1-\alpha_k ) x_k + \alpha_k Hx_k\]

Stochastic Approximation

What if $H$ is not completely known?

Even in the case when $H$ is not completely known, or $Hx_k$ itself cannot be calculated easily, we might still have access to a random observation $s_k$

\[s_k = Hx_k + w_k\]

along with the information about the random noise $w_k$.

In this case, we might try to iteratively update the equation using this observation!

\[\begin{aligned} x_{k+1} & = (1-\alpha_k) x_k + \alpha_k s_k \\ & = (1-\alpha_k) + \alpha_k (Hx_k + w_k) \end{aligned}\]

Stochastic Approximation Algorithm

Stochastic approximation algorithm starts from this point!

\[x_{k+1} = (1-\alpha_k) x_k + \alpha_k (Hx_k + w_k )\]

where $s_k = Hx_k +w_k$ can be available using sample data. It is known that, under certain conditions, the error between $s_k$ and $Hx_k$ can be neglected at the end. This is the key idea of stochastic approximation!

Robbins-Monro Algorithm

For instance, let’s consider the policy evaluation equation

\[\begin{aligned} v^\pi & = \mathcal{T}^\pi v^\pi\\ & = r(s,\pi(s) ) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p (s' \vert s, a) v(s') \ \end{aligned}\]

This can be expressed by the following form

\[x = \mathbb{E} [ f(x, \xi) ]\]

where $\xi$ is a random variable.

Let’s say we have a sample of $\xi$, ${\hat{\xi}_1, \hat{\xi}_2, \cdots, \hat{\xi}_N}$. Then, we might use an empirical mean to to estimate $\mathbb{E}[f(x,\xi)]$ in the iterative algorithm.

\[x_{k+1} := (1-\alpha_k) x_k + \alpha_k \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N f(x, \hat{\xi}_i )\]

However, this can be extremely inefficient when $N$ becomes large. Thus, we might want to use a simplified version: using a single sample $\hat{\xi}_i$ in each update.

\[x_{k+1} := (1-\alpha_k) x_k + \alpha_k f(x_k , \hat{\xi}_k )\]

This is called the Robbins-Monro algorithm. This is one of the special case of stochastic approximation method, with zero mean $w_k$.

Algorithm

The stochastic approximation algorithm for $Hx=x$ can be written as

\[\begin{aligned} x_{k+1}(i) & = (1-\alpha_k (i) ) x_k (i) + \alpha_k (i) [(Hx_k) (i) + w_k (i) ]\\ & = x_k (i) + \alpha_k (i) [ (Hx_k) (i) + w_k (i) - x_k (i) ] \end{aligned}\]

Thus, the step direction is $(Hx_k) (i) - x_k (i) + w_k (i)$. Also, we need to carefully choose the stepsize.

Stepsize Analysis

Let’s first assume that our noise term $w_k (i)$ has variance $\sigma^2$. We may also assume that $w_k$ is independent of $x_k$.

Attempt 1: Constant Stepsize

What if we use a constant step size of $\alpha_k (i) = \alpha$?

  • The variance of $x_{k+1}(i)$ will be greater than (or equal to) $\alpha^2 \sigma^2 $.

  • Thus, $x_k$ might possibly reach near the neighborhood of $x^\star$, but just randomly move around it, without conerging it to $x^\star$.

  • Therfore, the effect of noise term will not be diminished at end.

Attempt 2: Stepsize Decreasing so quickly

Now, let’s consider the case where the stepsize decreases so quickly that both

\[\sum_{k}^\infty \alpha_k (i) < +\infty\]

and

\[(Hx_k)(i) + w_k (i) - x_k (i) < +\infty\]

are bounded. Then,

\[|x_k(i) - x_0 (i) | \leq \sum_{\tau=0}^{k-1} | x_{\tau+1}(i) - x_\tau (i) | = \sum_{\tau=0}^{k-1}\alpha_\tau (i) |(Hx_\tau ) (i) - x_\tau (i) + w_\tau (i) |\]

we can see that $x_k(i)$ can never escape a fixed boundary around $x_0 (i)$. Thus, if $x_0$ is placed outside hte neighborhood of $x^\star$, $x_k$ can never reach at $x^\star$.

Thus, the noise must be large enough to explore the entire space of $H$.

Condition on Stepsizes

Assumptions For each $i$, the following conditions hold with probability 1.

\[\sum_{k=0}^\infty \alpha_k (i) = +\infty\\ \sum_{k=0}^\infty \alpha_k^2 (i) <+\infty\]

Condition on Noise

Define the history

\[\mathcal{F}_k:= \{ x_0, \cdots, x_k, w_0, \cdots, w_{k-1}, \alpha_0, \cdots, \alpha_k \}\]

Assumptions For each $i$, the following conditions hold:

\[\mathbb{E}[w_k (i) \ \vert \ \mathcal{F}_k ] =0\\ \mathbb{E}[w_k^2 (i) \ \vert \ \mathcal{F}_k ] \leq C_0 + C_1 \|x_k \|^2\]

for some costant $C_0, C_1$.

Convergence Theorem

Convergence under contraction property

Theorem If both of the assumptions hold and $H$ is a contraction mapping, $x_k$ converges to the fixed point $x^\star$ of $H$, with probability 1.

proof

Convergence under monotonicity

Assumptions Suppose that mapping $H$ satisfies the following conditions:

  1. $H$ is monotone
  2. There exists a unique vector $x^\star$ such that $Hx^\star = x^\star$.
  3. Let $\mathbf{1}$ be the vector with all components equal to 1. Then, \(Hx - \eta \mathbf{1} \leq H(x- \eta \mathbf{1}) \leq H(x+ \eta \mathbf{1}) \leq Hx + \eta \mathbf{1}\)

Theorem Suppose that all three assumptions hold. If the sequence $x_k$ generated by the stochastic approximation algorithm is bounded with probability 1, then $x_k$ converges to $x^\star$ with probability 1.

proof
entry.summary: Review: MDP Problem Bellman Equations Recall, that both policy evaluation and optimality equation is a fixed point problem. Policy Evaluation \[v = \mathcal{T}^\pi v\] or equivalently \[v(s) = r(s, \pi(s)) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \vert s, \pi(s) ) v(s')\] Optimality Equation \[v = \mathcal{T}v\] or equivalently \[v(s) = \max_{a\in A} \bigg [ r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \vert s,a) v(s') \bigg ]\] Thus, we need to solve a fixed-point problem in a model-free manner. Fixed Point Problem Consider the following system of equations: \[Hx = x\] where $ H: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^n $. We may want to use a deterministic iterative algorithm, \[x_{k+1} := Hx_k\] or its variant with a small stepsize $\alpha_k \in (0,1)$. \[x_{k+1} := (1-\alpha_k ) x_k + \alpha_k Hx_k\] Stochastic Approximation What if $H$ is not completely known? Even in the case when $H$ is not completely known, or $Hx_k$ itself cannot be calculated easily, we might still have access to a random observation $s_k$ \[s_k = Hx_k + w_k\] along with the information about the random noise $w_k$. In this case, we might try to iteratively update the equation using this observation! \[\begin{aligned} x_{k+1} & = (1-\alpha_k) x_k + \alpha_k s_k \\ & = (1-\alpha_k) + \alpha_k (Hx_k + w_k) \end{aligned}\] Stochastic Approximation Algorithm Stochastic approximation algorithm starts from this point! \[x_{k+1} = (1-\alpha_k) x_k + \alpha_k (Hx_k + w_k )\] where $s_k = Hx_k +w_k$ can be available using sample data. It is known that, under certain conditions, the error between $s_k$ and $Hx_k$ can be neglected at the end. This is the key idea of stochastic approximation! Robbins-Monro Algorithm For instance, let’s consider the policy evaluation equation \[\begin{aligned} v^\pi & = \mathcal{T}^\pi v^\pi\\ & = r(s,\pi(s) ) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p (s' \vert s, a) v(s') \ \end{aligned}\] This can be expressed by the following form \[x = \mathbb{E} [ f(x, \xi) ]\] where $\xi$ is a random variable. Let’s say we have a sample of $\xi$, ${\hat{\xi}_1, \hat{\xi}_2, \cdots, \hat{\xi}_N}$. Then, we might use an empirical mean to to estimate $\mathbb{E}[f(x,\xi)]$ in the iterative algorithm. \[x_{k+1} := (1-\alpha_k) x_k + \alpha_k \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N f(x, \hat{\xi}_i )\] However, this can be extremely inefficient when $N$ becomes large. Thus, we might want to use a simplified version: using a single sample $\hat{\xi}_i$ in each update. \[x_{k+1} := (1-\alpha_k) x_k + \alpha_k f(x_k , \hat{\xi}_k )\] This is called the Robbins-Monro algorithm. This is one of the special case of stochastic approximation method, with zero mean $w_k$. Algorithm The stochastic approximation algorithm for $Hx=x$ can be written as \[\begin{aligned} x_{k+1}(i) & = (1-\alpha_k (i) ) x_k (i) + \alpha_k (i) [(Hx_k) (i) + w_k (i) ]\\ & = x_k (i) + \alpha_k (i) [ (Hx_k) (i) + w_k (i) - x_k (i) ] \end{aligned}\] Thus, the step direction is $(Hx_k) (i) - x_k (i) + w_k (i)$. Also, we need to carefully choose the stepsize. Stepsize Analysis Let’s first assume that our noise term $w_k (i)$ has variance $\sigma^2$. We may also assume that $w_k$ is independent of $x_k$. Attempt 1: Constant Stepsize What if we use a constant step size of $\alpha_k (i) = \alpha$? The variance of $x_{k+1}(i)$ will be greater than (or equal to) $\alpha^2 \sigma^2 $. Thus, $x_k$ might possibly reach near the neighborhood of $x^\star$, but just randomly move around it, without conerging it to $x^\star$. Therfore, the effect of noise term will not be diminished at end. Attempt 2: Stepsize Decreasing so quickly Now, let’s consider the case where the stepsize decreases so quickly that both \[\sum_{k}^\infty \alpha_k (i) < +\infty\] and \[(Hx_k)(i) + w_k (i) - x_k (i) < +\infty\] are bounded. Then, \[|x_k(i) - x_0 (i) | \leq \sum_{\tau=0}^{k-1} | x_{\tau+1}(i) - x_\tau (i) | = \sum_{\tau=0}^{k-1}\alpha_\tau (i) |(Hx_\tau ) (i) - x_\tau (i) + w_\tau (i) |\] we can see that $x_k(i)$ can never escape a fixed boundary around $x_0 (i)$. Thus, if $x_0$ is placed outside hte neighborhood of $x^\star$, $x_k$ can never reach at $x^\star$. Thus, the noise must be large enough to explore the entire space of $H$. Condition on Stepsizes Assumptions For each $i$, the following conditions hold with probability 1. \[\sum_{k=0}^\infty \alpha_k (i) = +\infty\\ \sum_{k=0}^\infty \alpha_k^2 (i) <+\infty\] Condition on Noise Define the history \[\mathcal{F}_k:= \{ x_0, \cdots, x_k, w_0, \cdots, w_{k-1}, \alpha_0, \cdots, \alpha_k \}\] Assumptions For each $i$, the following conditions hold: \[\mathbb{E}[w_k (i) \ \vert \ \mathcal{F}_k ] =0\\ \mathbb{E}[w_k^2 (i) \ \vert \ \mathcal{F}_k ] \leq C_0 + C_1 \|x_k \|^2\] for some costant $C_0, C_1$. Convergence Theorem Convergence under contraction property Theorem If both of the assumptions hold and $H$ is a contraction mapping, $x_k$ converges to the fixed point $x^\star$ of $H$, with probability 1. proof Convergence under monotonicity Assumptions Suppose that mapping $H$ satisfies the following conditions: $H$ is monotone There exists a unique vector $x^\star$ such that $Hx^\star = x^\star$. Let $\mathbf{1}$ be the vector with all components equal to 1. Then, \(Hx - \eta \mathbf{1} \leq H(x- \eta \mathbf{1}) \leq H(x+ \eta \mathbf{1}) \leq Hx + \eta \mathbf{1}\) Theorem Suppose that all three assumptions hold. If the sequence $x_k$ generated by the stochastic approximation algorithm is bounded with probability 1, then $x_k$ converges to $x^\star$ with probability 1. proof

entry.title: [RL Theory] Linear Programming Approach
entry.content:
Iteration based vs Optimization based

Recall that the Bellman operator is given by

\[(\mathcal{T}v) (s) := \max_{a\in A} \bigg [ r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \vert s,a) v(s') \bigg ].\]

We tried to solve above Bellman equation by iteration-based methods.

In this section, we will try to solve the Bellman equation by Optimization-based method.

Optimization Based Method

Problem Formulation

Instead of the iteration-based algorithm, we can obtain the value function by solving:

\[\begin{aligned} \min_{v \in \mathbb{R}^n } & \ \ \ c^T v \\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ \ v \geq \mathcal{T}v \end{aligned}\]

where the inequality holds entry-wise, and each entry of $c$ is positive.

[Theorem] The optimization problem above has a unique optimal solution, which corresponds to the optimal value function $v^\star$.

[proof] For feasible $v$, \(v \geq \mathcal{T}v \geq \mathcal{T}^2 v \geq \cdots \geq \mathcal{T}^k v\)

Taking $k\rightarrow \infty$ for both sides, we have

\[v \geq v^\star\]

Multiplying $c^T$ at both sides, (since $c_i >0$ for all $i$)

\[c^T v \geq c^T v^\star\]

we can see that $v^\star$ which satisfies $v^\star = \mathcal{T}v^\star$ makes the objective function $c^Tv$ the smallest. Thus, $v^\star$ is the optimal solution.

Now, we also want to show that this optimal solution $v^\star$ is unique. First, let’s assume that other solution $\hat{v}$ exists, and $\hat{v} \not = v^\star$.

Since $\hat{v}$ is an optimal solution, there should be some $i$ that satisfies

\[\hat{v}(i) < v^\star (i) \ \ \ \exists i\]

or otherwise, $c^T v^\star = c^T \hat{v}$ cannot be satisfied ($c_i >0 $ for all $i$).

Meanwhile, $\hat{v}(i) \geq \mathcal{T}\hat{v}(i)$ holds for all $i$ since $\hat{v}$ is an optimal solution that satisfies the constraint. Applying this $k$ times and taking it to infinity, we have

\[\hat{v}(i) \geq v^\star (i) \ \ \ \forall i\]

This is in fact a contradiction!

Linear Programming Formulation

Note that the constraint $v\geq \mathcal{T}v$ is not a linear constraint. Fortunately, we can change it to a linear program.

\[\begin{aligned} \min_{v \in \mathbb{R}^n } & \ \ \ c^T v \\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ \ v(s) \geq r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \vert s, a) v(s') \ \ \ \forall (s,a) \in S \times A \end{aligned}\]

Now, the dimension of optimization variable is $n$, but the number of constraints is $n\times m$.

Evaluating Policy via LP

Given a policy $\pi$, consider

\[\begin{aligned} \min_{v \in \mathbb{R}^n } & \ \ \ c^T v \\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ \ v \geq \mathcal{T}^\pi v \end{aligned}\]

which its optimal solution is $v^\pi$.

This can also be formulated as an LP by:

\[\begin{aligned} \min_{v \in \mathbb{R}^n } & \ \ \ c^T v \\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ \ v(s) \geq r(s,\pi(s)) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \vert s, \pi(s)) v(s') \ \ \ \forall s \in S \end{aligned}\]

In this case, the dimension of optimization variable and the number of constraints are both $n$.

Dual Problem

Lagrangian

Note that our constraint’s dimension is $n\times m$, thus its corresponding dual variable is a function of $(s,a)$.

First, define the Lagrangian.

\[L(v, \lambda) = \sum_s c(s) v(s) + \sum_{s,a} \lambda (s,a) \bigg [ r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \vert s,a) v(s') - v(s) \bigg ]\]

Dual Function

Then, we have the dual function.

\[g(\lambda) = \min_v L(v,\lambda) = \sum_{s,a} \lambda (s,a) r(s,a)\]

when

\[c(s') + \gamma \sum_{s,a} \lambda(s,a) p(s' \vert s,a) - \sum_a \lambda (s', a) =0 \ \ \ \forall s' \in S\]

Dual Problem

We can then define our dual problem, which its duality gap is zero (it satisfies the slater’s condition)
entry.summary: Iteration based vs Optimization based Recall that the Bellman operator is given by \[(\mathcal{T}v) (s) := \max_{a\in A} \bigg [ r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \vert s,a) v(s') \bigg ].\] We tried to solve above Bellman equation by iteration-based methods. In this section, we will try to solve the Bellman equation by Optimization-based method. Optimization Based Method Problem Formulation Instead of the iteration-based algorithm, we can obtain the value function by solving: \[\begin{aligned} \min_{v \in \mathbb{R}^n } & \ \ \ c^T v \\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ \ v \geq \mathcal{T}v \end{aligned}\] where the inequality holds entry-wise, and each entry of $c$ is positive. [Theorem] The optimization problem above has a unique optimal solution, which corresponds to the optimal value function $v^\star$. [proof] For feasible $v$, \(v \geq \mathcal{T}v \geq \mathcal{T}^2 v \geq \cdots \geq \mathcal{T}^k v\) Taking $k\rightarrow \infty$ for both sides, we have \[v \geq v^\star\] Multiplying $c^T$ at both sides, (since $c_i >0$ for all $i$) \[c^T v \geq c^T v^\star\] we can see that $v^\star$ which satisfies $v^\star = \mathcal{T}v^\star$ makes the objective function $c^Tv$ the smallest. Thus, $v^\star$ is the optimal solution. Now, we also want to show that this optimal solution $v^\star$ is unique. First, let’s assume that other solution $\hat{v}$ exists, and $\hat{v} \not = v^\star$. Since $\hat{v}$ is an optimal solution, there should be some $i$ that satisfies \[\hat{v}(i) < v^\star (i) \ \ \ \exists i\] or otherwise, $c^T v^\star = c^T \hat{v}$ cannot be satisfied ($c_i >0 $ for all $i$). Meanwhile, $\hat{v}(i) \geq \mathcal{T}\hat{v}(i)$ holds for all $i$ since $\hat{v}$ is an optimal solution that satisfies the constraint. Applying this $k$ times and taking it to infinity, we have \[\hat{v}(i) \geq v^\star (i) \ \ \ \forall i\] This is in fact a contradiction! Linear Programming Formulation Note that the constraint $v\geq \mathcal{T}v$ is not a linear constraint. Fortunately, we can change it to a linear program. \[\begin{aligned} \min_{v \in \mathbb{R}^n } & \ \ \ c^T v \\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ \ v(s) \geq r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \vert s, a) v(s') \ \ \ \forall (s,a) \in S \times A \end{aligned}\] Now, the dimension of optimization variable is $n$, but the number of constraints is $n\times m$. Evaluating Policy via LP Given a policy $\pi$, consider \[\begin{aligned} \min_{v \in \mathbb{R}^n } & \ \ \ c^T v \\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ \ v \geq \mathcal{T}^\pi v \end{aligned}\] which its optimal solution is $v^\pi$. This can also be formulated as an LP by: \[\begin{aligned} \min_{v \in \mathbb{R}^n } & \ \ \ c^T v \\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ \ v(s) \geq r(s,\pi(s)) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \vert s, \pi(s)) v(s') \ \ \ \forall s \in S \end{aligned}\] In this case, the dimension of optimization variable and the number of constraints are both $n$. Dual Problem Lagrangian Note that our constraint’s dimension is $n\times m$, thus its corresponding dual variable is a function of $(s,a)$. First, define the Lagrangian. \[L(v, \lambda) = \sum_s c(s) v(s) + \sum_{s,a} \lambda (s,a) \bigg [ r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \vert s,a) v(s') - v(s) \bigg ]\] Dual Function Then, we have the dual function. \[g(\lambda) = \min_v L(v,\lambda) = \sum_{s,a} \lambda (s,a) r(s,a)\] when \[c(s') + \gamma \sum_{s,a} \lambda(s,a) p(s' \vert s,a) - \sum_a \lambda (s', a) =0 \ \ \ \forall s' \in S\] Dual Problem We can then define our dual problem, which its duality gap is zero (it satisfies the slater’s condition)

entry.title: [RL Theory] Policy Iteration
entry.content:
Policy Iteration

Idea of Policy Iteration

So far, our algorithm updated everything only in the value space. After we obtained the optimal value function $v^\star$, we switched over to the policy space, and obtained $\pi^\star$.

This can be considered somewhat inefficient: we might want to use some information about the policy as well, during the value function updating procedure. Policy Iteration iteratively updates both value functions and policies.

Policy Iteration Algorithm

  1. Initialize $\pi_0$ as an arbitrary deterministic stationary policy.

  2. Repeat

    • policy evaluation : compute the value function corresponding to the policy $\pi_k$!

      \[v^{\pi_k} = \mathcal{T}^{\pi_k} v^{\pi_k}\]

    • policy improvement : update the policy by taking

      \[\pi_{k+1} = \arg \max_{a\in A} \bigg [ r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'\in S} p(s' \vert s,a) v^{\pi_k }(s') \bigg ]\]

  3. Then the sequence of policies ${\pi_0 , \pi_1, \pi_2, \cdots, \pi_k}$ converges to an optimal policy $\pi^\star$.

This works: Monotonic Improvement

One might ask, does this really work? That question can be answered if we prove the following Lemma.

[Lemma] Let ${\pi_k}$ be the sequences of policies obtained by policy iteration. Then, we have

\[v^{\pi_{k+1}} \geq v^{\pi_k}\ \ \ \ \forall k\]

Note that this is an element-wise inequality!

\[v^{\pi_{k+1}}(s) \geq v^{\pi_k}(s)\ \ \ \ \forall k,s\]

[Proof]

First, the policy evaluation step gives us the solution of

\[v^{\pi_k} = \mathcal{T}^{\pi_k }v^{\pi_k}\]

Next, the policy improvement step tries to update the policy $\pi_k$ by solving

\[\pi_{k+1} = \arg \max_{a\in A} \bigg [ r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'\in S} p(s' \vert s,a) v^{\pi_k }(s') \bigg ]\]

Thus, the policy improvement step is in fact the same as an optimal Bellman operator, taking the argmax over action space.

\[\mathcal{T}^{\pi_{k+1}}v^{\pi_k } = \mathcal{T}v^{\pi_k }\]

Now, the definition (optimality) of Bellman operator gives us that

\[\mathcal{T}^{\pi_{k+1}}v^{\pi_k } = \mathcal{T}v^{\pi_k } \geq \mathcal{T}^{\pi_k }v^{\pi_k}\]

Combining the equation from the policy evaluation step, we have

\[\mathcal{T}^{\pi_{k+1}}v^{\pi_k } \geq \mathcal{T}^{\pi_k }v^{\pi_k} = v^{\pi_k}\]

If we apply this inequality recursively, we have

\[v^{\pi_k } \leq \big ( \mathcal{T}^{\pi_{k+1}}\big ) ^n v^{\pi_k }\ \ \ \ \forall n\]

Knowing that $\mathcal{T^{\pi}}$ is a contraction mapping, if we think of $n\rightarrow \infty$, we can say that the RHS goes to the fixed point of $\mathcal{T}^{\pi_{k+1}}$, which is

\[v^{\pi_k } \leq v^{\pi_{k+1}}\]

Thus, the policy is improved monotonically, as we repeat the policy iteration.

Convergence of Policy Iteration

We now know that policy iteration monotonically improves the policy, thus monotonically increases the value function. Now, we have to prove that it eventually converges to the optimal value function!

Monotone Convergence Theorem

Let ${x_k}$ be a monotonically non-decreasing, bounded sequence of real vectors. Then, the sequence has a finite limit!

We’re going to use this widely-used theorem to prove our point! Recall that our value function is bounded, since we have a finite state-action space.

Proof of Convergence

To start with, our reward function is bounded for some constant $M$, since our state-action spaces $S$ and $A$ are finite.

\[|r(s,a) | \leq M\]

Thus, our value function has to be bounded above!

\[|v^\pi (s)| = \bigg \vert \sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t r(s_t, a_t ) \bigg \vert \leq \bigg \vert \sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t |M| \bigg \vert \leq \frac{1}{1-\gamma}|M|\]

By the previous lemma, $v^{\pi_k} \leq v^{\pi_{k+1}}$ for all $k$. Then, by the monotone convergence theorem, there exists $\bar{v}$ such that

\[\lim_{k\rightarrow \infty} v^{\pi_k } = \bar{v}\]

Then, the preceding policy evaluation and improvement step

  • policy improvement : $\bar{v} \rightarrow \bar{\pi}$
  • policy evaluation : $\bar{\pi} \rightarrow v^{\bar{\pi}}$

implies that

\[v^{\bar{\pi}} = \mathcal{T}^{\bar{\pi}}v^{\bar{\pi}} = \mathcal{T} v^{\bar{\pi}}\]

thus, $\bar{v} = v^\star$.

VI vs PI

VI is simpler, but PI is often faster!

After we obtained the optimal value function $v^\star$, we switched over to the policy space, and obtained $\pi^\star$. This works: Monotonic Improvement One might ask, does this really work? That question can be answered if we prove the following Lemma. [Lemma] Let ${\pi_k}$ be the sequences of policies obtained by policy iteration. Then, we have \[v^{\pi_{k+1}} \geq v^{\pi_k}\ \ \ \ \forall k\] Note that this is an element-wise inequality! \[v^{\pi_{k+1}}(s) \geq v^{\pi_k}(s)\ \ \ \ \forall k,s\] [Proof] First, the policy evaluation step gives us the solution of \[v^{\pi_k} = \mathcal{T}^{\pi_k }v^{\pi_k}\] Next, the policy improvement step tries to update the policy $\pi_k$ by solving \[\pi_{k+1} = \arg \max_{a\in A} \bigg [ r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'\in S} p(s' \vert s,a) v^{\pi_k }(s') \bigg ]\] Thus, the policy improvement step is in fact the same as an optimal Bellman operator, taking the argmax over action space. \[\mathcal{T}^{\pi_{k+1}}v^{\pi_k } = \mathcal{T}v^{\pi_k }\] Now, the definition (optimality) of Bellman operator gives us that \[\mathcal{T}^{\pi_{k+1}}v^{\pi_k } = \mathcal{T}v^{\pi_k } \geq \mathcal{T}^{\pi_k }v^{\pi_k}\] Combining the equation from the policy evaluation step, we have \[\mathcal{T}^{\pi_{k+1}}v^{\pi_k } \geq \mathcal{T}^{\pi_k }v^{\pi_k} = v^{\pi_k}\] If we apply this inequality recursively, we have \[v^{\pi_k } \leq \big ( \mathcal{T}^{\pi_{k+1}}\big ) ^n v^{\pi_k }\ \ \ \ \forall n\] Knowing that $\mathcal{T^{\pi}}$ is a contraction mapping, if we think of $n\rightarrow \infty$, we can say that the RHS goes to the fixed point of $\mathcal{T}^{\pi_{k+1}}$, which is \[v^{\pi_k } \leq v^{\pi_{k+1}}\] Thus, the policy is improved monotonically, as we repeat the policy iteration.
